does: This paper introduces a multilingual alignment framework that addresses the problem of training AI systems to reduce both global and local harms across diverse languages and cultural contexts. The authors collect the first annotated dataset of red-teaming prompts in multiple languages, explicitly distinguishing between universal harms and culture-specific harms. They evaluate various alignment approaches including preference training and safety measures against this multilingual evaluation set, demonstrating how standard Western-centric alignment techniques often fail or transfer poorly across languages and regions. The work establishes empirical evidence that preference distributions for harmful content vary significantly across geographies, requiring fundamentally different approaches than homogeneous monolingual safety alignment.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining limitations of standard AI safety alignment, which has historically focused on English and Western preferences. It extends recent work on pluralistic alignment and values alignment by introducing explicit geographic and linguistic dimensions. The paper engages with preference learning and RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) literature, showing how these methods overfit to Western-centric datasets. It also connects to cross-cultural NLP research examining how language models behave differently across languages, and draws on red-teaming methodologies that have become standard in AI safety. The research responds to critiques that alignment often implicitly optimizes for narrow preference distributions rather than acknowledging genuine value pluralism across global communities.
enables: This work opens new research directions in genuinely global AI alignment, moving beyond the implicit assumption that a single set of safety principles should govern systems used worldwide. It enables development of alignment methods that can navigate genuine preference conflicts across cultures without simply defaulting to Western norms. The multilingual red-teaming dataset and harm taxonomy create infrastructure for evaluating whether future alignment techniques truly serve diverse global users or merely appear to through surface-level localization. This foundation supports work on value-aware training, geographic-aware adaptation strategies, and methods for identifying when global and local preferences genuinely conflict. The framework also enables better understanding of how to construct AI systems that respect legitimate pluralism while maintaining safeguards against universally harmful content.
